coredata.dc:title: A miniature electrochemical detection system based on GOQDs/MWCNTs /SPCE* for determination the purine in cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Shi
Guo, Xiaoling
Meng, Lingren
Cui, Jiwen
Li, Jinlian
Yuan, Xing
Wu, Dongmei
coredata.dc:description: The development of a simple, portable and accurate instrument used in the evaluation of in vitro cytotoxicity is particularly important and urgent in the field of toxicology research at present. In the current study, a miniature electrochemical detection system was constructed that integrated graphene oxide quantum dots and multiwall carbon nanotubes modified anodized screen printed carbon electrode (GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*) with a mini reaction vessel, which reduced the consumption of sample from 500 to 80 μL. Four electrochemical signals could be detected in cells distinctly for the first time which were attributed to the oxidation of uric acid, guanine/xanthine, adenine and hypoxanthine, respectively. This miniature electrochemical detection system has better selectivity, sensitivity and its detection limits were lower than those of most electrochemical sensors. Furthermore it has been found that the level of purine nucleotide metabolism in BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells was different, which could be related to the different purine nucleotide metabolisms of cancer cells and non cancer cells. The simple, portable and miniature electrochemical detection system could be used as a convenient instrument for toxicology detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Miniature electrochemical detection system
Purine nucleotide metabolism
Cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior of UA and purine on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*
The selectivity and sensitivity of GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*
Quantification of UA and purine in cells by the miniature electrochemical detection system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the (A) SPCE*, (B) MWCNTs/SPCE*, (C) GOQDs/SPCE*, and (D) GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*.

Electrochemical response of a mixture of 5 μM UA, 10 μM G, 10 μM X, 20 μM A and 20 μM HX on (a) SPCE, (b) SPCE*, (c) MWCNTs/SPCE*, (d) GOQDs/SPCE* and (e) GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*. Inset: Electrochemical response of (a) mixture of 5 μM UA, 10 μM G, 10 μM X, 20 μM A and 20 μM HX, (b) 5 μM UA, (c) 10 μM G, (d) 10 μM X, (e) 20 μM A and (f) 20 μM HX on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*.

Electrochemical response of (A) UA (0.08, 0.17, 0.34, 0.50, 0.67, 1.00, 1.70, 2.50, 3.40, 4.20, 5.00, 5.90, 6.70 μg/mL) in the presence of 10 μM G, 10 μM X, 20 μM A and 20 μM HX; (B) G (0.08, 0.15, 0.30, 0.45, 0.60, 0.90, 1.70, 2.70, 3.30, 4.50 μg/mL) in the presence of 5 μM UA, 20 μM A and 20 μM HX; (C) X (0.08, 0.23, 0.45, 0.75, 1.10, 1.20, 2.30, 2.70, 3.80, 4.50 μg/mL) in the presence of 5 μM UA, 20 μM A and 20 μM HX; (D) A (0.13, 0.40, 0.80, 1.20, 2.00, 2.70, 3.25, 3.80, 4.30, 5.40, 6.10 μg/mL) in the presence of 5 μM UA, 10 μM G, 10 μM X and 20 μM HX and (E) HX (0.13, 0.40, 0.68, 1.10, 1.50, 2.00, 2.85, 4.10, 4.75, 5.40, 6.10, 6.80 μg/mL) in the presence of 5 μM UA, 10 μM G, 10 μM X and 20 μM A on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*. Supporting electrolyte: 0.2 M PBS (pH 7.4). Accumulation time: 240 s.

(A) Electrochemical response of (a) BALB/3T3 and (b) MCF-7 cell suspension on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* after a baseline correction. (B) HPLC chromatogram of (a) BALB/3T3 and (b) MCF-7 cell suspension. Cell concentration: 1 × 106 cells mL−1.
Comparison of the proposed method with other electrochemical methods found in the literature.
Contents of UA and purine in cells (mean, n = 6, μg/mL).

head.ce:title: A miniature electrochemical detection system based on GOQDs/MWCNTs /SPCE* for determination the purine in cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130117, Jilin, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Development and Evaluation of the Efficacy of Toxicology, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi, 154007, Heilongjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Development and Evaluation of the Efficacy of Toxicology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of a simple, portable and accurate instrument used in the evaluation of in vitro cytotoxicity is particularly important and urgent in the field of toxicology research at present. In the current study, a miniature electrochemical detection system was constructed that integrated graphene oxide quantum dots and multiwall carbon nanotubes modified anodized screen printed carbon electrode (GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*) with a mini reaction vessel, which reduced the consumption of sample from 500 to 80 μL. Four electrochemical signals could be detected in cells distinctly for the first time which were attributed to the oxidation of uric acid, guanine/xanthine, adenine and hypoxanthine, respectively. This miniature electrochemical detection system has better selectivity, sensitivity and its detection limits were lower than those of most electrochemical sensors. Furthermore it has been found that the level of purine nucleotide metabolism in BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells was different, which could be related to the different purine nucleotide metabolisms of cancer cells and non cancer cells. The simple, portable and miniature electrochemical detection system could be used as a convenient instrument for toxicology detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Cell electrochemistry applies the principles and experimental methods of electrochemistry [1,2], to reveal the relationship between cellular structure and function and the effect that exogenous molecules have on cells [3–5]. In recent years, cell electrochemistry as a new technique has attracted much attention, and has been used in toxicity testing by detecting the changes of intracellular purine contents at the molecular level [6–8], for instance, the screening of anticancer drugs [9], the effect of environmental estrogens [10], and the cytotoxicity detection of typical environmental pollutants (heavy metals, chlorophenols, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, etc.) [11–13].

The electrochemical signal of red blood cells at 0.73 V has been found for the first time in 1999 by Yun-Xiang Ci et al. [14] and was attributed to the oxidation of guanine by Huang Xian Ju [15] six years later. In 2009, the electrochemical signal at about 0.70 V was observed as a mixed-signal and it was attributed to the oxidation of intracellular guanine and xanthine in our study [16]. In 2012, another mixed-signal at about 1.05 V was found, which was attributed to the oxidation of intracellular adenine and hypoxanthine [17]. However, it was impossible to evaluate the cell activity related to purine nucleotide metabolism through mixed electrochemical signals of purine in cells [18]. In recent years, we have been trying to find independent signals of four purines. In 2014, the electrochemical signals of cellular adenine and hypoxanthine could be detected independently by reduced graphene oxide and ionic liquid modified glass carbon electrode [19], but the electrode was complicated to prepare and it could be used only once, and time-consuming activation was needed for the next detection. In 2015, the electrochemical signals of cellular guanine and xanthine could be detected independently in acidic condition (pH 2.0) on polyoxometalate modified 3D graphene foam [20], but the strong acid testing condition destroyed the normal physiological environment of cells. Then the individual concentrations of guanine, xanthine, adenine and hypoxanthine in cells could be obtained by enzyme-assisted electrochemical detecting system that introduced xanthine oxidase [21]. Nevertheless, its widespread use was limited by its disadvantages of long analysis time, high cost of enzyme and need of large quantity of cell sample. Therefore, it was important to build a simple, miniature and multiple signals electrochemical detecting system for in vitro toxicological evaluation.

Screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) [22] are disposable carbon electrodes and are easy to miniaturize in a small reactor [23], such as in blood glucose meter and uric acid meter [24–26]. In this paper, we constructed a miniature electrochemical detection system based on graphene oxide quantum dots and multiwall carbon nanotubes modified anodized screen printed carbon electrode (GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*). The detection limits and linear range of UA and purine by this system were compared with those of previous electrochemical sensors. The electrochemical behavior of BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells was studied over a wide potential window, and the contents of UA and purine in cells were verified by HPLC method. The simple, portable and miniature electrochemical detection system is expected to be a convenient instrument for toxicology detection.

Uric acid (UA), guanine (G), xanthine (X), adenine (A) and hypoxanthine (HX) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) were bought from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd., China. Screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE, SE100) was purchased from Zensor R&D, Taiwan. High-purity multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were acquired from Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd., China. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared by mixing 0.2 M KH2PO4 with 0.2 M Na2HPO4. All other reagents were of analytical grade and double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurement was carried out with CHI 660 electrochemical workstation. A three-electrode system was used consist of SPCE working electrode, Ag/AgCl/KClsat reference electrode and a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, which were assembled in a miniature electrochemical detection system shown in Graphical Abstract. The size of the reaction vessel was matched with SPCE working electrode, the reference electrode and auxiliary electrode were tied up with a rubber band, and 80 μL samples was needed in this reactor.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained by SU-8010 Hitachi electron microscope. The process of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was referred to reported literature [11].

Prior to combined with GOQDs [27], the MWCNTs were purified as previously described [21]. There were many carboxylic acid moieties at the edge of purified MWCNTs, which made it easy to functionalize with various inorganic, organic or biological molecules [28]. GOQDs/MWCNTs solution was achieved by mixing 0.2 mg/mL GOQDs and 1 mg/mL purified MWCNTs under vigorous stirring (Fig. S3).

A SPCE was pre-anodized in PBS (pH 7.4) by applying an anodic potential of +1.8 V for 300 s then designated as SPCE*, The GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* was prepared by dropping 10 μL of GOQDs/MWCNTs solution on the surface of SPCE* and then dried under an infrared lamp. For comparison, 10 μL GOQDs solution and MWCNTs solution were respectively dropped on the surface of SPCE* then obtained GOQDs/SPCE* and MWCNTs/SPCE*.

BALB/3T3, MCF-7 cell lines were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. The method of cell culture and the in situ cell collection method were referring to the process reported previously [19].

The morphology of SPCE*, MWCNTs/SPCE*, GOQDs/SPCE*, and GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* was investigated by SEM analysis and the results were presented in Fig. 1 . There were a great content of carbon particles and disordered distribution graphite sheets on SPCE (Fig. S1) [30]. The SPCE* (Fig. 1A) was noticed with porous structure, which might be attributed to the oxygen functional group generated on the surface of the SPCE* [31]. As compared with SPCE, the SPCE* was easy to modify and had a better hydrophilicity due to the porous structure. It may be noted that network-like structure of MWCNTs were observed on the surface of MWCNTs/SPCE* (Fig. 1B). In Fig. 1C, the porous structure of SPCE* have been covered with a film of GOQDs. Fig. 1D showed that GOQDs were incorporated into MWCNTs matrix to form a rougher surface than the MWCNTs, indicated that the GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* have been prepared successfully.

Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) measurement was used to evaluate the charge transport properties of the five different modified electrodes. In EIS, the semicircle diameter could represent the electron-transfer resistance (R
ct ), which dominated the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode interface or electrolyte interface [32]. As shown in Fig. S2, the SPCE (curve a) had a larger semicircle, indicating greater interface impedance. After pre-anodized in PBS solution, the semicircle became smaller due to the good electrical conductivity of SPCE* (curve b). Compared with SPCE*, the value of R
ct in GOQDs/SPCE* (curve c) decreased slightly, which demonstrated that the existence of GOQDs could decrease the resistance of the sensing platform between the electrodes/solution interfaces. Although GOQDs could promote direct electron transfer, their conductivity was not good enough as they behave as semiconductors [33]. The value of R
ct in MWCNTs/SPCE* (curve d) decreased dramatically compared with SPCE* and GOQDs/SPCE*, which implied the facile electron transfer on MWCNTs/SPCE* due to the existence of MWCNTs. As to GOQDs/CMWCNTs/SPCE* (curve e), the value of R
ct was much less than that of GOQDs/SPCE*, which proved that the MWCNTs could act as an excellent electrochemical platform for electron transfer between GOQDs and SPCE* [29]. The results of EIS supported that the GOQDs/CMWCNTs solution was successfully prepared and modified on the SPCE* surface. This also strongly proved that the GOQDs/CMWCNTs/SPCE* could be a promising electrochemical platform for sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of UA and purine on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the mixture of UA and purine (included G, X, A, and HX) on different electrodes were displayed in Fig. 2 . There was nearly no peak of the mixed solution on SPCE (curve a), which has no response to UA and purine. A clear electrochemical signal at 0.29 V that attributed to the oxidation of UA (Fig. 2-inset, curve b) could be detected on GOQDs/SPCE* (curve d) and GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* (curve e), but could not on SPCE* and MWCNTs/SPCE*. The electrochemical signal at 0.66 V attributed to the oxidation of G and X (Fig. 2-inset, curve c and d) detected on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* (curve e) was the strongest. The electrochemical signals at 0.93 V and 0.98 V that attributed to the oxidation of A and HX (in Fig. 2- inset, curve e and f) could be distinguished on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* (curve e), respectively. According to the variation of electrochemical signals on different electrodes, it was believable that the proposed GOQDs/CMWCNTs/SPCE* have been fabricated successfully. The above results also indicated that the GOQDs/MWCNTs composites had an excellent electrocatalytic activity towards the oxidation of UA and purine, which were mainly attributed to three factors: (1) the excellent electrical conductivity of MWCNTs [34], (2) the high electroactive surface area of oxygen-rich functional groups GOQDs and (3) the strong π–π interaction could be formed easily between GOQDs and MWCNTs due to the similar conjugated structures [35]. The MWCNTs/GOQDs composites integrated the excellent properties of GOQDs and MWCNTs. A simple and miniature electrochemical detection system based on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* was prepared, which was expected to be a convenient instrument used in the detection of biological specimen instantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and sensitivity of GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE*
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* was investigated in the mixture of UA and purine when the concentration of one substance changed, whereas the concentrations of the rest four substances were kept constant. As shown in Fig. 3 , the peak currents of UA, G, X, A and HX were increased with their concentrations increasing, respectively. Furthermore, the electrochemical signals of other four compounds with constant concentration did not showed obvious change. The above results confirmed that the electrochemical signals of UA, G/X, A and HX on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* were well separated when they co-exist. The electrochemical behavior for different concentrations of UA and purine were not affected under the existence of other four substances. The calibration parameters of UA, G, X, A and HX were listed in Table S1, and the detection limits of UA, G, X, A and HX on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* were lower than the reported shown in Table 1 [36–40]. Compared the results of miniature electrochemical detection system with those of most electrochemical sensors in the determination of UA and purine, the electrochemical detection system was linear related in the low concentration region and with lower detection limits in the determination of UA and purine. Although the electrochemical signal of G/X could not been separated, the miniature electrochemical detection system with higher selectivity and sensitivity was inexpensive, simple, portable and accurate, which has a good prospect of application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of UA and purine in cells by the miniature electrochemical detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
As an important kind of non cancer cells, BALB/3T3 cells played a significant role in the study of carcinogens. MCF-7 cells as hormone dependence cancer cells were often used in the effect of environmental estrogens. Then BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells were selected as cellular models, and the miniature electrochemical detection system and HPLC assay were used for determination the UA and purine in cells as seen in Fig. 4
. Four electrochemical signals at 0.28, 0.65, 0.92, 0.97 V were detected in BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells for the first time (Fig. 4 A). Compared with UA and purine, four electrochemical signals could be attributed to the oxidation of UA, G/X, A and HX, respectively.

The results of HPLC (Fig. 4 B) showed that, five chromatographic peaks at 8.67, 13.82, 15.22, 28.07 and 12.25 min (Fig.5 4 curve b) were accordance with UA and purine, indicating the existence of UA, G, X, A and HX in MCF-7 cells. However, only four chromatographic peaks at 8.64, 13.87, 15.36 and 12.28 min (Fig. 4 B curve a) attributed to UA, G, X and HX were detected in BALB/3T3 cells.

The contents of UA and purine in cells were calculated by the linear regression equations of five substances and shown in Table 2 . The contents of UA, A and HX in BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells by electrochemical method were similar to the HPLC analysis. It was worth noting, the content of A could not be detected by HPLC in BALB/3T3 cells, 0.35 μg/mL of A was detected by the electrochemical system. The results indicated that it was feasible and effective to determine the contents of UA and purine in cells by this miniature electrochemical detection system, which has better sensitivity especially in UA and A. Furthermore, the level of purine nucleotide metabolism in the two types of cells was different. BALB/3T3 cells showed low level expression of purine and high level expression of UA as compared with that of MCF-7 cells, which may related to the different purine metabolisms between cancer cells and non cancer cells. This miniature electrochemical detection system provided an alternative approach for further study on purine nucleotide metabolism of cells.

A miniature electrochemical detection system based on GOQDs/MWCNTs/SPCE* was constructed, and the consumption of sample was greatly reduced from 500 μL in traditional electrochemical system to 80 μL that significantly reduced the amount of cells. Four electrochemical signals attributed to uric acid, guanine/xanthine, adenine and hypoxanthine could be detected in cells for the first time, and especially the electrochemical signals of cellular adenine and hypoxanthine were detected separately. This miniature electrochemical detection system had better selectivity, sensitivity and its detection limits were lower than those of most electrochemical sensors. Furthermore, the level of purine nucleotide metabolism in BALB/3T3 cells and MCF-7 cells has been found different by this miniature electrochemical detection system, which provided technical support for the further analysis of the differences of purine metabolisms between cancer cells and non cancer cells at the molecular level. Therefore, this system was expected to be a convenient instrument and used in the detection of toxicology or the physiological process related to purine nucleotide metabolism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledged the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777060 and 51178092), Nature Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (qc2017119), Basic Scientific Research Project for Heilongjiang Provincial Colleges and Universities (2017-KYYWF-0591), Heilongjiang Province Leading Talent Echelon Reserve Leader Fund, Jiamusi University National Project Cultivation Project (JMSUJCGP2016-001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2019.04.015.
